title: Berlin Ghost Stations: Quick Summary
items: **Fifteen Berlin stations were sealed on 13 August 1961** and stayed shut until after the Wall fell. They sat on three routes that ran mostly through West Berlin but dipped under East Berlin: the U6, the U8 and the north-south S-Bahn tunnel, plus Bornholmer Straße above ground.

**The trains kept running, they just did not stop.** West Berlin passengers rolled slowly past bricked-up, dimly lit platforms guarded by armed men. Berliners called them Geisterbahnhöfe, ghost stations.

**Friedrichstraße was the one exception.** It stayed open inside East Berlin as a sealed transfer point, a bit like changing planes without entering the country. The Tränenpalast next door was the border hall, it is free to enter, and it is closed on Mondays.

**Almost nothing is left to see on the platforms.** Fourteen of the fifteen are ordinary working stations today, including Alexanderplatz, Naturkundemuseum and Stadtmitte. Do not go hunting for abandoned ruins.

**Nordbahnhof is the one stop worth planning.** Its mezzanine holds a free permanent exhibition on the border and ghost stations, open during normal S-Bahn hours, and the Berlin Wall Memorial on Bernauer Straße is directly above it. Do both as one visit.

**One of the fifteen is closed again.** Französische Straße on the U6 shut permanently on 3 December 2020 when Unter den Linden opened as the new U5/U6 interchange. Nothing to do with the Cold War this time.
